entries.title: Composite chain expansion for reusable tool sequences
entries.description:
# Composite chain expansion for reusable tool sequences

## Context

Captured during development session on 2026-02-21

## Pattern

Composites in chains/composites.yaml define reusable sequences that expand to multiple atomic chains at runtime. Example: design-component expands to [shadcn-available, component-exists, dev-rules, architecture-patterns, component-workflow, contrast]. gather-build-knowledge expands to vault search + dev rules + architecture patterns. Composites prevent duplication across flows — changing a composite updates all flows that reference it. Expansion is recursive (composites can contain other composites).

## Example

```typescript
composites.yaml: design-component: [shadcn-available, component-exists, dev-rules, architecture-patterns, component-workflow, contrast]
```

## Why

Without composites, each flow would duplicate the same 6-chain sequence. Composites provide single-point-of-change for shared workflows.

entries.title: Pre-weighted keyword matching for O(n) intent classification
entries.description:
# Pre-weighted keyword matching for O(n) intent classification

## Context

Captured during development session on 2026-02-21

## Pattern

Intent classification uses pre-computed keyword weights from routing-config.yaml instead of runtime NLP. Each intent has a keyword list with static weights (CREATE: 1.0, ENHANCE: 0.9 to avoid confusion with CREATE). Classification is O(n) string matching against the keyword list, plus regex semantic patterns for contextual signals. This avoids LLM inference costs for routing decisions. The tradeoff is potential substring collisions (e.g., 'implement' matching inside 'implementation').

## Example

```typescript
classifyIntent('Create a Button') → keyword 'create' matched (weight 1.0) + semantic pattern matched (weight 0.85) → intent: CREATE, score: 0.925
```

## Why

LLM-based intent classification adds latency and cost to every request. Pre-weighted keywords give deterministic, fast, auditable routing.

entries.title: Returning empty array for all failure modes
entries.description:
# Returning empty array for all failure modes

## Context

Functions that scan directories or read files

## Anti-Pattern

Returning [] for 'not found', 'empty', and 'error' states makes it impossible to distinguish between normal (nothing to do) and abnormal (broken config) situations.

## Example (Bad)

```typescript
// BAD:
function getHooks(dir) {
  if (!fs.existsSync(dir)) return []; // Not found
  try { return fs.readdirSync(dir); }
  catch (err) { return []; } // Error - same as not found!
}
```

## Why It's Wrong

User sees 'No hooks found' but doesn't know if directory is missing, empty, or unreadable. Can't troubleshoot without knowing which case applies.

## Correct Approach

See: [detailed-status-returns](../../patterns/other/detailed-status-returns.md)
